message:
scripts/kconfig/confdata.c: Fix buffer overflow and format truncation warnings

Replace sprintf with snprintf and add path length checks to fix compiler warnings
about potential buffer overflows. The warnings occurred in three locations:

1. Line 119: Path concatenation using sprintf
2. Line 771: Configuration file path concatenation
3. Line 774: Temporary filename creation

The fix ensures that path lengths are properly validated before concatenation,
preventing potential buffer overflows while avoiding format truncation warnings.

triage.reason: The patch replaces unsafe sprintf calls with snprintf and adds explicit length checks before writing into fixed-size buffers, directly preventing out-of-bounds writes. This is a clear security fix addressing CWE-787. The change is confined to a single source file and constitutes a coherent, single-fix modification.

message:
l2tp: fix buffer overflow and type errors in Calling/Called Number handling

  Fix issues introduced in 88a2ebdb:

  - Fix type declaration: uint8_t *calling[254] declared an array of 254
    pointers instead of an array of 254 bytes. Remove erroneous asterisks.

  - Fix buffer overflow vulnerability: L2TP AVP values can be up to 1017
    bytes (L2TP_AVP_LEN_MASK - sizeof(avp_header)), but buffers were only
    254(*4?) bytes. A malicious packet could cause stack buffer overflow.
    Use L2TP_AVP_LEN_MASK (1023) for buffer size to handle maximum AVP length.

  - Remove useless NULL checks: Stack-allocated arrays can never be NULL,
    causing compiler warnings. The existence check is n > 0 / m > 1.

Signed-off-by: Denys Fedoryshchenko <denys.f@collabora.com>

triage.reason: The commit explicitly fixes a stack‑based buffer overflow by increasing the size of a stack‑allocated array to accommodate the maximum AVP length (up to 1017 bytes). The change replaces an undersized array with a correctly sized one, directly addressing the overflow risk described in CWE‑121. The patch is small, focused, and modifies only a few lines, indicating a localized fix.

message:
vlib: fix vlib_buffer_free_inline() AVX-512 overflow

'queue' is declared on the stack and must be big enough to handle
queue_size + max processed elements per loops.
When AVX-512 was added, this additional max was not increased from 4 to
8 accordingly.

Type: fix
Fixes: 6f7832e5f6

Change-Id: I15f2f930fc316f92da2e4642f41852d856c06a48
Signed-off-by: Benoît Ganne <bganne@cisco.com>

triage.reason: The patch increases the size of a stack‑allocated array (queue) to prevent an overflow that occurs when AVX‑512 processes more elements. This directly addresses a stack‑based buffer overflow vulnerability, matching CWE‑121. The change is a small, single‑file fix, satisfying the localization criteria.

message:
bpf, array: fix heap out-of-bounds access when updating elements

During own review but also reported by Dmitry's syzkaller [1] it has been
noticed that we trigger a heap out-of-bounds access on eBPF array maps
when updating elements. This happens with each map whose map->value_size
(specified during map creation time) is not multiple of 8 bytes.

In array_map_alloc(), elem_size is round_up(attr->value_size, 8) and
used to align array map slots for faster access. However, in function
array_map_update_elem(), we update the element as ...

memcpy(array->value + array->elem_size * index, value, array->elem_size);

... where we access 'value' out-of-bounds, since it was allocated from
map_update_elem() from syscall side as kmalloc(map->value_size, GFP_USER)
and later on copied through copy_from_user(value, uvalue, map->value_size).
Thus, up to 7 bytes, we can access out-of-bounds.

Same could happen from within an eBPF program, where in worst case we
access beyond an eBPF program's designated stack.

Since 1be7f75d1668 ("bpf: enable non-root eBPF programs") didn't hit an
official release yet, it only affects priviledged users.

In case of array_map_lookup_elem(), the verifier prevents eBPF programs
from accessing beyond map->value_size through check_map_access(). Also
from syscall side map_lookup_elem() only copies map->value_size back to
user, so nothing could leak.

  [1] http://github.com/google/syzkaller

Fixes: 28fbcfa08d8e ("bpf: add array type of eBPF maps")
Reported-by: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>

triage.reason: The commit fixes a heap out-of-bounds write in the eBPF array map implementation. The original code used a rounded-up element size for memcpy, causing up to 7 bytes to be written past the allocated buffer. The patch changes the memcpy length to the actual map->value_size, eliminating the OOB write. This is a clear security fix that maps directly to CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write). The change is confined to a single file with a one‑line modification, satisfying the localization criterion.

message:
hexdump-parse: handle truncated format pattern

If the fmt being parsed by block_size is exactly '%', *++fmt is the
terminator and strchr will return a valid pointer to the terminator of
spec rather than NULL, the while condition will pass and subsequent
strchr will read past the end of fmt until a spec character is found
again

ASAN aborts with the following error on the first buffer overread:

AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow on address 0x602000000212 at pc 0x55bf1c4b2d78 bp 0x7ffe33c8cff0 sp 0x7ffe33c8cfe0
READ of size 1 at 0x602000000212 thread T0
    #0 0x55bf1c4b2d77 in block_size ../text-utils/hexdump-parse.c:207
    #1 0x55bf1c4ad36f in main ../text-utils/hexdump.c:214
    #2 0x7f15f063c78f  (/usr/lib/libc.so.6+0x2378f)
    #3 0x7f15f063c849 in __libc_start_main (/usr/lib/libc.so.6+0x23849)
    #4 0x55bf1c4ac6c4 in _start (../build/hexdump+0x86c4)

triage.reason: The commit addresses a heap-buffer-overflow caused by reading past the end of a format string in `block_size`. The added check `&& *fmt != '\0'` prevents the over-read, which is a classic example of CWE-126 (Buffer Over-read). The change is confined to a single source file and constitutes a single, coherent fix.

message:
Fix critical security vulnerabilities in ml_gdkpixbuf.c

Comprehensive security hardening based on code review addressing memory
safety, integer overflow, and GC interaction issues.

Critical Fixes:
1. Document intentional memory leak in ml_raise_gdkpixbuf_error()
   - caml_failwith() copies string before raising exception
   - Small bounded leak on error paths is acceptable
   - Alternative would be truncation risk with stack buffer

2. Add malloc() NULL checks in convert_gdk_pixbuf_options()
   - Check allocation success before dereferencing
   - Raise out_of_memory exception on failure
   - Properly clean up partial allocations

3. Add integer overflow protection in convert_gdk_pixbuf_options()
   - Check: len > (SIZE_MAX / sizeof(char*)) - 1
   - Prevents overflow in: sizeof(char*) * (len + 1)
   - Raises invalid_argument exception if exceeded

4. Add bounds checking to list_length()
   - Validate list cell structure with Is_block() and Wosize_val()
   - Detect malformed/corrupted lists
   - Prevent infinite loops on cyclic lists
   - Check for counter overflow (UINT_MAX)

5. Add CAMLparam/CAMLlocal to ml_gdkpixbuf_savefunc()
   - Function calls caml_alloc_string() which can trigger GC
   - Function calls caml_callback_exn() which can trigger GC
   - Proper GC root registration prevents use-after-free
   - Use CAMLreturnT for non-value return type

Additional Hardening:
6. Add validation for option pair structure
   - Check Is_block() and Wosize_val() >= 2
   - Prevents memory corruption from malformed data

7. Add defensive NULL buffer check in ml_gdkpixbuf_savefunc()
   - Validate buf != NULL when count > 0
   - Guards against library contract violations

8. Add #include <limits.h> for SIZE_MAX and UINT_MAX
   - Required for overflow checks

9. Add comprehensive comments explaining security decisions
   - Document why memory leak is acceptable
   - Explain GC safety requirements
   - Note defensive checks and their purpose

Security Impact:
- Prevents heap buffer overflow (CVE-worthy)
- Prevents segfault on malloc failure
- Prevents infinite loops on malformed data
- Prevents GC-related use-after-free
- Maintains compatibility with existing tests (25/25 pass)

All tests still passing after security fixes.

triage.reason: The commit introduces multiple memory‑safety checks (malloc NULL validation, integer overflow protection for allocation size, list length and structure validation, buffer NULL checks, and proper GC root handling). These changes directly prevent out‑of‑bounds writes/reads and heap buffer overflows, which are the core of CWE‑119. The changes are confined to a single source file (ml_gdkpixbuf.c), making the fix localized and clearly a security‑focused remediation.

message:
pass1: fix buffer overflow from setbuf size mismatch

On Linux, setbuf() assumes the buffer is BUFSIZ (8192 bytes), but sbuf
is only 512 bytes. This caused writes to overflow into adjacent globals
(numbuf, then cmst), corrupting the expression stack.

Use setvbuf() with explicit size on Linux to avoid the overflow.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

triage.reason: The commit explicitly addresses a buffer overflow caused by using setbuf with a buffer smaller than the assumed BUFSIZ. The fix replaces setbuf with setvbuf and provides the correct size, preventing writes beyond the stack-allocated buffer. This is a clear remediation of a stack‑based buffer overflow, matching CWE‑121. The change is a single, coherent patch affecting a small number of files.

Reducer: add check on received data to avoid segfault (#152143)

When ncclCommAbort is called it may return invalid/corrupted data to the reducer. This adds a check so we don't read past the end of the tensors leading to a segfault.

message:
avcodec/libopenh264dec: Increase array sizes, fix stack-buffer overread

av_image_copy() expects an array of four pointers and linesizes
according to its declaration; it currently only pointers that are
actually in use (depending upon the pixel format), but this might
change at any time. It has already happened for the linesizes in
d7bc52bf456deba0f32d9fe5c288ec441f1ebef5 and so increasing their
array fixes a stack-buffer overread.

This fixes a -Wstringop-overflow= and -Wstringop-overread warning
from GCC 11.2.

Reviewed-by: Linjie Fu <linjie.justin.fu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Andreas Rheinhardt <andreas.rheinhardt@outlook.com>
(cherry picked from commit b0b90100bfda8c5cf95889e00183589de0abce60)

message:
Pad Coeff and Clip buffers to 8 elements in ALF 5x5 unit_test

Pad Coeff and Clip buffers to 8 elements in ALF 5x5 unit_test to
prevent ASan overflow in simdFilter5x5Blk.

The overflow only occurs in the x86 path (simdFilter5x5Blk), which uses
'_mm_loadu_si128((__m128i*)filterSet)' to load eight shorts even though
'MAX_NUM_ALF_CHROMA_COEFF' is defined as seven. The same applies to the
clip buffer. In the unit test, 'filterSet'/'fClipSet' were 7-element
vectors, so the eighth load causes ASan heap buffer overflow.

In the real decoder, the coefficient buffer comes from
'AlfSliceParam::chromaCoeff' (and similarly 'chromaClipp'), which is a
8×7 array inside the struct. When 'altIdx == 7', that extra load lands
in the next slot (struct padding/next field), keeping it in-bounds
within the struct's storage and thus never flagged at runtime.

triage.reason: The commit fixes an out‑of‑bounds SIMD load that caused an AddressSanitizer heap buffer overflow by padding the coefficient and clip buffers from 7 to 8 elements. This directly addresses an improper restriction of operations within the bounds of a memory buffer, which maps to CWE‑119. The change is small, confined to a single test file, and clearly a security‑related fix.

message:
Avoid memory size overflow when allocating backend activity buffer

The code in charge of copying the contents of PgBackendStatus to local
memory could fail on memory allocation because of an overflow on the
amount of memory to use.  The overflow can happen when combining a high
value track_activity_query_size (max at 1MB) with a large
max_connections, when both multiplied get higher than INT32_MAX as both
parameters treated as signed integers.  This could for example trigger
with the following functions, all calling pgstat_read_current_status():
- pg_stat_get_backend_subxact()
- pg_stat_get_backend_idset()
- pg_stat_get_progress_info()
- pg_stat_get_activity()
- pg_stat_get_db_numbackends()

The change to use MemoryContextAllocHuge() has been introduced in
8d0ddccec636, so backpatch down to 12.

Author: Jakub Wartak
Discussion: https://postgr.es/m/CAKZiRmw8QSNVw2qNK-dznsatQqz+9DkCquxP0GHbbv1jMkGHMA@mail.gmail.com
Backpatch-through: 12

triage.reason: The commit addresses an integer overflow in the calculation of the allocation size for a backend activity buffer. The overflow could cause the allocated buffer to be smaller than required, leading to out-of-bounds reads when the buffer is later filled. The fix casts operands to a larger type and uses a allocation routine that can handle huge sizes, directly mitigating the OOB read risk. The change is small, localized, and clearly a security fix targeting CWE-125.

message:
BUG/MEDIUM: mux-quic: fix crash on invalid fctl frame dereference

Emission of flow-control frames have been recently modified. Now, each
frame is sent one by one, via a single entry list. If a failure occurs,
emission is interrupted and frame is reinserted into the original
<qcc.lfctl.frms> list.

This code is incorrect as it only checks if qcc_send_frames() returns an
error code to perform the reinsert operation. However, an error here
does not always mean that the frame was not properly emitted by lower
quic-conn layer. As such, an extra test LIST_ISEMPTY() must be performed
prior to reinsert the frame.

This bug would cause a heap overflow. Indeed, the reinsert frame would
be a random value. A crash would occur as soon as it would be
dereferenced via <qcc.lfctl.frms> list.

This was reproduced by issuing a POST with a big file and interrupt it
after just a few seconds. This results in a crash in about a third of
the tests. Here is an example command using ngtcp2 :

 $ ngtcp2-client -q --no-quic-dump --no-http-dump \
   -m POST -d ~/infra/html/1g 127.0.0.1 20443 "http://127.0.0.1:20443/post"

Heap overflow was detected via a BUG_ON() statement from qc_frm_free()
via qcc_release() caller :

  FATAL: bug condition "!((&((*frm)->reflist))->n == (&((*frm)->reflist)))" matched at src/quic_frame.c:1270

This does not need to be backported.

triage.reason: The commit fixes a bug that could cause a heap overflow by inserting a random frame into a list without checking if the temporary list is empty. The added LIST_ISEMPTY guard prevents the erroneous insertion, directly addressing the heap-based buffer overflow described. This is a clear security fix that maps to CWE-122, and the change is a small, self‑contained patch.

message:
nilfs2: fix OOB in nilfs_set_de_type

[ Upstream commit c4a7dc9523b59b3e73fd522c73e95e072f876b16 ]

Fix CVE: CVE-2024-26981

The size of the nilfs_type_by_mode array in the fs/nilfs2/dir.c file is
defined as "S_IFMT >> S_SHIFT", but the nilfs_set_de_type() function,
which uses this array, specifies the index to read from the array in the
same way as "(mode & S_IFMT) >> S_SHIFT".

static void nilfs_set_de_type(struct nilfs_dir_entry *de, struct inode
 *inode)
{
	umode_t mode = inode->i_mode;

	de->file_type = nilfs_type_by_mode[(mode & S_IFMT)>>S_SHIFT]; // oob
}

However, when the index is determined this way, an out-of-bounds (OOB)
error occurs by referring to an index that is 1 larger than the array size
when the condition "mode & S_IFMT == S_IFMT" is satisfied.  Therefore, a
patch to resize the nilfs_type_by_mode array should be applied to prevent
OOB errors.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20240415182048.7144-1-konishi.ryusuke@gmail.com
Reported-by: syzbot+2e22057de05b9f3b30d8@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Closes: https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=2e22057de05b9f3b30d8

message:
sys_dup: Add Phase 5 FD bounds validation

VULNERABILITY: Out-of-Bounds FD Table Access

ATTACK SCENARIO:
Attacker provides FD value exceeding task->max_fds
- Task has max_fds = 1024 (typical limit)
- Attacker calls dup(9999)
- Syscall validates oldfd >= 0 (passes)
- vfs_get_file_from_task accesses fd_table[9999]
- If fd_table array has < 9999 entries → OOB read
- Kernel crashes or reveals memory contents

IMPACT:
- Information disclosure: OOB read reveals kernel memory contents
- Kernel crash: Accessing unmapped memory causes page fault
- Undefined behavior: OOB access corrupts adjacent structures
- Potential privilege escalation: Reading function pointers or credentials

ROOT CAUSE:
Line 132 validated oldfd >= 0 but not oldfd < max_fds
- Lower bound check present, upper bound missing
- vfs_get_file_from_task may not validate bounds internally
- Syscall layer must ensure oldfd is within valid range

DEFENSE (Phase 5):
- Validate oldfd < task->max_fds before any FD table access
- Check upper bound immediately after lower bound check
- Return -EBADF if oldfd >= max_fds
- Prevents OOB access to fd_table array
- Fail-fast before calling vfs_get_file_from_task

CVE REFERENCES:
- CVE-2014-0181: Linux fget out-of-bounds FD access
- CVE-2016-0728: Android FD table bounds violation

POSIX REQUIREMENT:
IEEE Std 1003.1-2017 dup(): "shall fail with EBADF if oldfd is
not a valid file descriptor"

PRECEDENT:
- sys_close Phase 5: Documents FD bounds validation responsibility
- sys_dup2 will need same validation for both oldfd and newfd
patch:
@@ -128,12 +128,64 @@ long sys_dup(int oldfd) {
         return -ESRCH;
     }
 
-    /* Phase 2: Validate oldfd early */
+    /* Phase 5: Validate oldfd bounds to prevent FD table out-of-bounds access
+     * VULNERABILITY: Out-of-Bounds FD Table Access
+     *
+     * ATTACK SCENARIO:
+     * Attacker provides FD value exceeding task->max_fds
+     * 1. Task has max_fds = 1024 (typical limit)
+     * 2. Attacker calls dup(9999)
+     * 3. Syscall validates oldfd >= 0 (passes)
+     * 4. Line 159: vfs_get_file_from_task accesses fd_table[9999]
+     * 5. If fd_table array has < 9999 entries → OOB read
+     * 6. Line 168: Loop searches fd_table up to max_fds (valid)
+     * 7. Line 201: task->fd_table[newfd] = old_file (valid if newfd < max_fds)
+     * 8. But initial vfs_get_file_from_task OOB read can crash kernel
+     *
+     * IMPACT:
+     * - Information disclosure: OOB read reveals kernel memory contents
+     * - Kernel crash: Accessing unmapped memory causes page fault
+     * - Undefined behavior: OOB access corrupts adjacent structures
+     * - Potential privilege escalation: Reading function pointers or credentials
+     *
+     * ROOT CAUSE:
+     * Line 132: Validates oldfd >= 0 but not oldfd < max_fds
+     * - Lower bound check present, upper bound missing
+     * - vfs_get_file_from_task may not validate bounds internally
+     * - Syscall layer must ensure oldfd is within valid range
+     *
+     * DEFENSE (Phase 5):
+     * Validate oldfd < task->max_fds before any FD table access
+     * - Check upper bound immediately after lower bound check
+     * - Return -EBADF if oldfd >= max_fds
+     * - Prevents OOB access to fd_table array
+     * - Fail-fast before calling vfs_get_file_from_task
+     *
+     * CVE REFERENCES:
+     * - CVE-2014-0181: Linux fget out-of-bounds FD access
+     * - CVE-2016-0728: Android FD table bounds violation
+     *
+     * POSIX REQUIREMENT:
+     * IEEE Std 1003.1-2017 dup(): "shall fail with EBADF if oldfd is
+     * not a valid file descriptor" - Requires bounds validation
+     *
+     * PRECEDENT:
+     * - sys_close Phase 5: Documents FD bounds validation responsibility
+     * - sys_dup2 will need same validation for both oldfd and newfd
+     */
     if (local_oldfd < 0) {
         fut_printf("[DUP] dup(oldfd=%d) -> EBADF (negative oldfd)\n", local_oldfd);
         return -EBADF;
     }
 
+    /* Phase 5: Validate oldfd upper bound */
+    if (local_oldfd >= task->max_fds) {
+        fut_printf("[DUP] dup(oldfd=%d, max_fds=%d) -> EBADF "
+                   "(oldfd exceeds max_fds, Phase 5: FD bounds validation)\n",
+                   local_oldfd, task->max_fds);
+        return -EBADF;
+    }
+
     /* Phase 2: Categorize oldfd range */
     const char *oldfd_category;
     if (local_oldfd <= 2) {
@@ -200,9 +252,9 @@ long sys_dup(int oldfd) {
     /* Assign the file to the new FD */
     task->fd_table[newfd] = old_file;
 
-    /* Phase 2: Detailed success logging */
+    /* Phase 5: Detailed success logging */
     fut_printf("[DUP] dup(oldfd=%d [%s]) -> %d [%s] (refcount=%u, "
-               "lowest available FD, Phase 2)\n",
+               "lowest available FD, Phase 5: FD bounds validation)\n",
                local_oldfd, oldfd_category, newfd, newfd_category, old_file->refcount);
 
     return newfd;

triage.reason: The patch adds an upper‑bound check for the file descriptor before accessing the task's fd_table, directly preventing an out‑of‑bounds read that could disclose kernel memory or cause a crash. This is a clear security fix addressing CWE‑125 (Out‑of‑bounds Read). The change is confined to a single source file and consists of a focused validation addition, satisfying the localization criterion.

message:
Fix RTSBindMessage OOB read on zero-length body

RTSBindMessage::UnpackContent() was reading the status byte directly
from m_Content without validating that the received payload actually
contains data. An attacker sending an RTS_BIND message with body_size=0
could cause an out-of-bounds read.

Add CalculateReceiveContentSize validation before accessing the status
byte, consistent with other RTS message handlers (RTSPolyDataMessage,
RTSTrackingDataMessage, RTSQuaternionTrackingDataMessage).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

message:
Fixed unvalidated CpuIndex (Arg2) in SMM_START_AP_PROC can cause out of bound writes (#21)

Bug Description:
The supervisor handles the SMM_START_AP_PROC call index. It calls the gMmCoreMmst.MmStartupThisAp() function pointer with 3 arguments provided by userspace. The exact function pointer points to SmmStartupThisAp().

The second argument (Arg2) is seen as a CpuIndex and is used as an index into an array that is being written too. There are no bounds checks to ensure the index is <= the number of CPUs present. In this case, user-space gets to provide an unvalidated index and write user-controlled values almost anywhere in memory.

Fix:
Add CpuIndex validation inside the dispatcher when handling SMM_START_AP_PROC.

fixes #10

triage.reason: The commit adds a bounds check on the CpuIndex argument before it is used as an index into an array that is written to. This directly prevents an out‑of‑bounds write caused by an unchecked index, which matches CWE‑787 (Out‑of‑bounds Write). The change is a small, single‑purpose patch, satisfying the localization requirement.

message:
MdePkg: Fix a buffer overread.

DevPathToTextUsbWWID allocates a separate copy of the SerialNumber
string to append a null terminator if the original string is not null
terminated.  However, by using AllocateCopyPool, it tries to copy
'Length + 1' words from the existing string containing 'Length'
characters into the target string.  Split the copy out to only copy
'Length' characters instead.

This was reported by GCC's -Wstringop-overread when compiling a copy
of this routine included in a library on FreeBSD.

Signed-off-by: John Baldwin <jhb@FreeBSD.org>

triage.reason: The commit explicitly fixes a buffer overread by changing the copy length from Length+1 to Length, preventing reading past the source string. This directly addresses an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). The change is a small, single‑file patch, satisfying the localization requirement.

message:
UPSTREAM: accel/qaic: tighten bounds checking in encode_message()

There are several issues in this code.  The check at the start of the
loop:

	if (user_len >= user_msg->len) {

This check does not ensure that we have enough space for the trans_hdr
(8 bytes).  Instead the check needs to be:

	if (user_len > user_msg->len - sizeof(*trans_hdr)) {

That subtraction is done as an unsigned long we want to avoid
negatives.  Add a lower bound to the start of the function.

	if (user_msg->len < sizeof(*trans_hdr))

There is a second integer underflow which can happen if
trans_hdr->len is zero inside the encode_passthrough() function.

	memcpy(out_trans->data, in_trans->data, in_trans->hdr.len - sizeof(in_trans->hdr));

Instead of adding a check to encode_passthrough() it's better to check
in this central place.  Add that check:

	if (trans_hdr->len < sizeof(trans_hdr)

The final concern is that the "user_len + trans_hdr->len" might have an
integer overflow bug.  Use size_add() to prevent that.

-	if (user_len + trans_hdr->len > user_msg->len) {
+	if (size_add(user_len, trans_hdr->len) > user_msg->len) {

Fixes: 129776ac2e38 ("accel/qaic: Add control path")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@linaro.org>
Reviewed-by: Pranjal Ramajor Asha Kanojiya <quic_pkanojiy@quicinc.com>

message:
net: qlogic/qede: fix potential out-of-bounds read in qede_tpa_cont() and qede_tpa_end()

[ Upstream commit 896f1a2493b59beb2b5ccdf990503dbb16cb2256 ]

The loops in 'qede_tpa_cont()' and 'qede_tpa_end()', iterate
over 'cqe->len_list[]' using only a zero-length terminator as
the stopping condition. If the terminator was missing or
malformed, the loop could run past the end of the fixed-size array.

Add an explicit bound check using ARRAY_SIZE() in both loops to prevent
a potential out-of-bounds access.

Found by Linux Verification Center (linuxtesting.org) with SVACE.

Fixes: 55482edc25f0 ("qede: Add slowpath/fastpath support and enable hardware GRO")
Signed-off-by: Pavel Zhigulin <Pavel.Zhigulin@kaspersky.com>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20251113112757.4166625-1-Pavel.Zhigulin@kaspersky.com
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Sasha Levin <sashal@kernel.org>

triage.reason: The commit adds explicit bounds checking to loops that iterate over a fixed-size array, preventing a potential out-of-bounds read when the terminating condition is missing or malformed. This directly addresses an out-of-bounds read vulnerability, which maps to CWE-125. The change is limited to a few source files and is a single, coherent fix.

message: fix vulkan read out of bounds warning of 0-stride vertex streams

triage.reason: The commit explicitly fixes a Vulkan out-of-bounds read warning caused by zero‑stride vertex streams. The added loop ensures offsets are set to zero when stride is zero, preventing the driver from reading past buffer bounds. This addresses an out-of-bounds read vulnerability, matching CWE-125. The change is small, localized to a single file, and clearly documented as a security‑related fix.

message:
Fix buffer overflow in Flatten lib

Missing null-terminator in extended pointer name triggers ASAN in OTs

This prevents subsequent code from reading past the end of the buffer (a classic buffer over‑read scenario). The change is small, confined to a single file, and directly addresses the missing null‑terminator issue, matching CWE‑126.

message:

This change prevents reading past the end of the allocated buffer, which matches the definition of an out‑of‑bounds read (CWE‑125). The modification is small, confined to a single file, and directly fixes the issue, indicating a clear security‑related fix.

message: Fix buffer overflow

triage.reason: The commit explicitly fixes a buffer overflow by increasing the size of static char arrays to include space for a null terminator and adjusting the strncpy length to avoid writing past the end of the buffers. This directly addresses an out-of-bounds write vulnerability, matching CWE-787. The change is limited to a single source file with a small, coherent patch.

message: Fix off-by-one error in loop condition

triage.reason: The patch changes a loop condition from `i <= d` to `i < d`, correcting an off‑by‑one error that would write to `gradients[d]` outside the allocated buffer of size `d`. This directly addresses an out‑of‑bounds write, which is a classic instance of CWE‑119 (Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer). The change is small, confined to a single file, and clearly a security‑related fix.

message:

message:

Fix heap buffer overflow when C-strings are shorter than NameData
types.

message: Fix off-by-one errors

message: Fixed constant array bounds check regression

triage.reason: The commit fixes a regression in a constant array bounds check by correcting the condition used to verify that an offset stays within the object's size. The previous check used multiplication, which could allow out‑of‑bounds reads/writes, while the new check uses addition, properly enforcing the buffer limit. This directly addresses an improper restriction of operations within a memory buffer, i.e., a buffer overflow/over‑read vulnerability, which maps to CWE‑119. The change is small, confined to a single file, and clearly a security‑related fix.

message:
xilpm: versal_common: server: Prevent out-of-bounds read when handling device attributes

The function was accessing Args[10] and Args[11] without ensuring
they exist, leading to potential out-of-bounds reads when
NumArgs < 12.
This fix ensures that the function only processes attributes if
NumArgs == 12, preventing partial population of DevAttr and ensuring
data integrity.

Signed-off-by: Ronak Jain <ronak.jain@amd.com>
Acked-by: Nicole Baze <amanda.baze@amd.com>

triage.reason: The commit explicitly addresses an out-of-bounds read by adding a check that ensures the Args array is accessed only when NumArgs == 12, preventing reads beyond the buffer. This directly maps to CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read). The change appears limited to a single function/file, satisfying the localization criterion.

message:
hw/net/smc91c111: Sanitize packet length on tx

When the smc91c111 transmits a packet, it must read a control byte
which is at the end of the data area and CRC.  However, we don't
sanitize the length field in the packet buffer, so if the guest sets
the length field to something large we will try to read past the end
of the packet data buffer when we access the control byte.

As usual, the datasheet says nothing about the behaviour of the
hardware if the guest misprograms it in this way.  It says only that
the maximum valid length is 2048 bytes.  We choose to log the guest
error and silently drop the packet.

This requires us to factor out the "mark the tx packet as complete"
logic, so we can call it for this "drop packet" case as well as at
the end of the loop when we send a valid packet.

Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Resolves: https://gitlab.com/qemu-project/qemu/-/issues/2742
Signed-off-by: Peter Maydell <peter.maydell@linaro.org>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@linaro.org>
Message-ID: <20250228174802.1945417-3-peter.maydell@linaro.org>
[PMD: Update smc91c111_do_tx() as len > MAX_PACKET_SIZE]
Signed-off-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@linaro.org>
(cherry picked from commit aad6f264add3f2be72acb660816588fe09110069)
Signed-off-by: Michael Tokarev <mjt@tls.msk.ru>

triage.reason: The patch adds a length check for transmitted packets and drops packets whose length exceeds the defined maximum. This prevents reading the control byte past the end of the packet buffer, eliminating a potential out‑of‑bounds read/over‑read. This directly addresses the improper restriction of operations within the bounds of a memory buffer, which maps to CWE‑119. The change is confined to a single source file with a small number of modifications, satisfying the localization criterion.
